author: Ogini Bernard
message:
Omo!! It’s still hard to put words together. Where do I even begin? Was it your constant guidance, or the care and passion you showed as a father?

You made sure all your children were academically grounded, and you taught us discipline, integrity, and the value of hard work. Right now, all I can truly say is thank you for being such a strong example to me, especially.

It still feels unreal. I thank God I saw you before you left, and that I was able to say goodbye, even though I never imagined it would be the last.

Please keep watching over us from above, Daddy. Extend my greetings to Saint Bernard and Saint Francis, and tell them I’m trying my best.

Thank you once again. Till we meet again. It is well. 🤍
excerpt: Omo!! It’s still hard to put words together. Where do I even begin? Was it your constant guidance, or the care and passion you showed as ...

author: Christopher Ololo
message:
**Tribute to Late Pharm. Sir F. F. Ogini*

**With deep reverence and gratitude to God, we celebrate the life and legacy of Sir F. F. Ogini—a distinguished Pharmacist, an exemplary leader, and a devoted servant of God*

Sir Francis Femi Ogini was a towering figure in the Pharmaceutical profession, 
*serving with honor as: "Chairman of the Pharmaceutical Association of Nigeria, Oyo State Chapter". His leadership was marked by integrity, excellence, and an unwavering commitment to the advancement of healthcare and professional standards.*

Beyond his professional accomplishments, *Sir Ogini’s life was a shining testimony of steadfast faith and dedicated service to the Catholic Church. 
*"A staunch Catholic and committed member of the Catholic Men Organization (CMO) of Sacred Heart Parish Ilupeju, Bodija*, he embodied the virtues of humility, discipline, and selfless service.

His contributions to the Church were both pioneering and transformative. *He served as a former "Metropolitan Grand Knight (MGK) of the Knights of St Mulumba, covering Ibadan Archdiocese & Oyo, Ekiti, Ondo, Osogbo and Ilorin Dioceses, where his guidance and mentorship inspired many*. 

*As the "Pioneer President of the League of Lay Readers in the Catholic Archdiocese of Ibadan, he helped strengthen liturgical participation and lay involvement in the Church.*

Sir Ogini also served as the "*a member of the Parish Pastoral Council and Chairman of Finance Commission and also the Coordinator of Parish Renew faith sharing of St. Richard’s Catholic Church, Jericho, Eleyele under Mongr T.O.A Fadeyi as the Parish Priest" ,* where he fostered unity and spiritual growth among parishioners. 

His visionary leadership continued as the 
*"Pioneer Parish Pastoral Council Chairman of Sacred Heart Parish, Ilupeju Bodija 2015 to 2018, laying a solid foundation for enduring pastoral development.*

*A pillar of the St John Paul 2 Society, Sacred Heart Parish, Ilupeju*

*He won the Award of a distinguished "Elderstateman" during the Celebration of the 10th Anniversary of the CMO of Sacred Heart Parish Ilupeju Bodija in 2019.*

Notably, he was the *"Pioneer Chairman of the Organizing Committee of the Resurrection Outreach, an Archdiocesan program, hosted by St. Richard’s Catholic Church, Eleyele*—a spiritually impactful program that *resonated throughout the Ibadan Archdiocese between 1996 and 1999*, touching countless lives and strengthening the faith of many.

*He was also the Archdiocese of Ibadan Chairman Ordination Planning Committee in 2003 at which 7 Priests were ordained among which is the AICMO Chaplain, Very Rev Fr Emmanuel Ajewole.*

*Sir F. F. Ogini’s life was one of purpose, service, and unwavering devotion—to his profession, his Church, and his community. His legacy remains a beacon of inspiration, reminding us all of the profound impact of a life well lived in service to God and humanity.*

*He will greatly be missed*

May his soul rest in perfect peace.

*Christopher Ololo*
Vice President,
Archdiocese of Ibadan Catholic Men Organization (AICMO)
excerpt: **Tribute to Late Pharm. Sir F. F. Ogini* **With deep reverence and gratitude to God, we celebrate the life and legacy of Sir F. F. Ogini...

author: Diana odey
message:
Diana Odey 

Tribute to my Dad. 

Dad, Dad, 
I write this tribute with mixed feelings. Where do I start from;an epitome of goodness, an apostle of peace, a lesson in humility, a paragon of modesty, my gist partner. You were so filled with lots of reality stories and I will not forget in my lifetime our last discussions just days before you passed. The substance of the gist will strengthen me for as long as I live. You believed in me and trusted my decisions. Continue to watch over us all as you take your well deserved rest. Adieu Daddy!

author: Sir Michael Mojoyinola
message:
Tribute from Sir M  O MOJOYINOLA.                  

My brother and my In-law , as I used to call you, your EXIT was sudden but thank God, it was peaceful. No wonder you have been an epitome of peace since I knew you in 1994.

I remember when you were  a big supplier of PPD Drugs and your workers colluded  to   steal  drugs and left  empty cartons, ,you  let it go un prosecuted . As a Knight, you were diligent, disciplined, thorough and committed.   You were the 2nd Supreme Officer from the  Ibadan  Metropolitan area.  When the KSM Degree  Exam was introduced,  you were the first  to champion coaching  for the same in Ibadan Metro Area.    We cannot forget inspiring  topics  in the weekly RENEW PROGRAMS in Ibadan KSM Platform.  Our last discussion some days before your demise  was that we should not stress ourselves  unnecessarily as what would be will be.
excerpt: Tribute from Sir M O MOJOYINOLA. My brother and my In-law , as I used to call you, your EXIT was sudden but thank God, it was peaceful. N...

author: Evelyn Etsiame
message:
Dear Daddy,
You were more than a father to me — you were my shelter, my defender, my quiet miracle on earth. I called you “Assistant Jesus” because whenever life became too heavy, somehow you always appeared with strength, wisdom, provision, comfort, or protection exactly when I needed it.

Your love felt safe. Your presence felt like home. Even your silence carried reassurance that I was never alone. You stood by me in ways words may never fully capture, and the emptiness your absence leaves behind is proof of how deeply you were loved.

You carried burdens without complaint, sacrificed without announcement, and loved without conditions. You taught me resilience, kindness, dignity, and endurance simply by the way you lived your life.

The world may know you as Sir F.F Ogini, but to me, you were my “Assistant Jesus” — because God used you repeatedly to answer prayers I never even spoke out loud.

Though my heart aches, I remain grateful that I had the privilege of being your child. Thank you for every sacrifice, every prayer, every protection, every embrace, and every moment you stood in the gap for me.

You may be gone from my sight, but never from my heart. Your voice still echoes in my memories, your lessons still guide my steps, and your love still surrounds me.

Rest well, Daddy.
You were deeply loved.
You are deeply missed.
And you will forever remain a part of me.
excerpt: Dear Daddy, You were more than a father to me — you were my shelter, my defender, my quiet miracle on earth. I called you “Assistant Jesu...

author: Olubunmi Irene Mojoyinola
message:
TO MY LOVING FATHER, 

Dad,  where do I start from? You left me so sooner than I expected. I remember that faithful day I was called on the phone that you were gone. The world was at a stand still, I was filled with grief that you left me fully prepared to be with our lord. The next word that came out of my mouth was MY SAINT HAS GONE. My dad did not teach me how to live, he lived like a saint and let me watch him do it. He was a man of peace. 

No matter how stormy a situation was he survived it and never was he consumed with bitterness. He carried a heavy invisible cross for years yet he chose grace over retaliation every single time. His life was a testament to the fact that you can be surrounded by hardship and still live a legacy of pure gold. My father's heart was always wanting to be with our God. He spoke of it not with fear, but with a joyous attitude. He loved our Lord so much to a point of no return. my Dad can never finish a sentence without him mentioning how he longs to be with our Lord. It became his spiritual anthem. He never looked for his reward or peace on his earthly days, he always looked up to heaven. Daddy I could write and write and keep writing but words cannot and would never fathom the depth of who you were. Today, the heavy crosses are gone, the silent storms are over and the quiet gallant warrior is finally at a perfect rest. You have finished your race, fulfilled. You have crossed the finish line and you are exactly where you always long to be ... TO BE WITH YOUR CREATOR. But leaving me to myself to grasp the reality of your absence is a weight for me. 

Dad i promise you that I will follow in your footsteps; I will carry myself with the same quiet dignity you showed when life was unfair. I promise to choose Grace over bitterness .To be a safe Harbor for those I love and to keep my eyes fixed on the higher external ground you always preached about. Rest well Dad. 
YOUR RACE IS OVER BUT YOUR LEGACY LIVES ON.
excerpt: TO MY LOVING FATHER, Dad, where do I start from? You left me so sooner than I expected. I remember that faithful day I was called on the ...

author: Olorunfemi Francis Ogini II
message:
NOW I UNDERSTAND

We spoke often about the future, about everything we both hoped to achieve, even as far ahead as five years from the very  last moment. I shared my dreams, my plans, and my expectations for life with so much certainty. You listened quietly every time, smiling gently as I spoke. Not because you did not believe in me, but because you already knew something I did not.

NOW I UNDERSTAND that while I was planning life ahead, you were quietly preparing for your departure. You never allowed your pain to become my burden. You carried that knowledge with grace, protecting me even while you were slowly fading within.

Whenever conversations drifted toward death, especially your own, I would quickly change the subject. I did not want to hear it. I wanted to keep you in my version of reality, the one where there was still time. 

Looking back now, NOW I UNDERSTAND I should have listened more carefully. I should have allowed you to finish those difficult sentences. Maybe then I would have asked the questions that now live forever inside me unanswered. I was not ready. But somehow, you were.

You spoke to me about legacy. You told me to name my first son after you, and I did. You wanted the family name and lineage to continue. You also named one of my daughters, Mimo, Mimonioluwa. Every time you spoke to her on the phone, you never started with ordinary words. You would sing first:

“Mimo loluwa... Mimo...”

Only after singing would you begin the conversation. That small act, repeated over and over, became your signature, your quiet language of love.

NOW I UNDERSTAND, as I continue to hear stories about the depth of your life and the greatness of your accomplishments. Yet you lived so simply, so genuinely, so deeply spiritual, and so unbothered by worldly noise.

Your Sunday calls never began without one important question:

“Did you go to church today?”

Sometimes, before answering your calls, I would begin preparing excuses in my mind if I had not gone. And I confess now, that sometimes I lied.

You may have appeared frail on the outside, but inside you were a warrior, disciplined, resilient, faithful, and strong in ways many people never fully saw. I peep through the paths you once walked and begin to see the giant strides you left behind, footsteps so large they feel almost impossible to fill.

There is still so much I wish I had asked. So much I wish I had understood while you were here. But even now, I carry you with me, in every prayer, every lesson, every memory, and every attempt to live with the quiet strength you carried so naturally.

And now, at last, I still DO NOT UNDERSTAND.
excerpt: NOW I UNDERSTAND We spoke often about the future, about everything we both hoped to achieve, even as far ahead as five years from the ver...

author: Courtney
message: My condolences goes out to the family in time of your loss. May God give you guys peace.

author: Deborah Egwim
message:
At the age of 79, Sir Ogini, you departed this world, leaving behind a legacy that words can scarcely capture. He was more than a parishioner; he was a pillar of our parish, a father figure, a counselor, and a steadfast servant in the vineyard of the Lord. His commitment to the growth and unity of the Church was evident in his daily life, his presence at parish activities, and the love he showed to everyone around him.

Sir, you lived a life rooted in Christian values. You served faithfully, gave selflessly, and carried yourself with dignity and kindness. Many of us will remember your calm wisdom, gentle smile, encouraging words, and unwavering devotion to the things of God. Your life touched countless souls, and his impact will continue to live on in the hearts of those privileged to know him.

You were a man who believed strongly in community, peace, and love. Through his actions, he taught us the importance of faithfulness, perseverance, and compassion. Even in difficult times, he remained devoted to God and committed to the well-being of others, we celebrate a life well spent. You fought the good fight, kept the faith, and finished his race honorably. We take comfort in the assurance that his labor in the Lord was not in vain.
 May the beautiful memories he left behind continue to inspire us to live lives of service, humility, and devotion.

Farewell, Sir Ogini.
Your service to God and humanity will never be forgotten.
May your gentle soul rest peacefully in the bosom of the Lord. Amen.
excerpt: At the age of 79, Sir Ogini, you departed this world, leaving behind a legacy that words can scarcely capture. He was more than a parishi...

author: Agboola Opeyemi Olatunji
message: Rest on Dad till we meet again
excerpt: Rest on Dad till we meet again

author: Lady B. Ogini (Wife)
message:
To My Dearest Husband,
Sir (Pharm.) Femi Francis Ogini
KSM, FPSN  

Your exit came as a great shock and a deeply painful one. Yet, I give God all the glory and honor for the 48 beautiful years we spent together.

You were a loving and caring husband, a devoted father to your children, and a wonderful grandfather to your grandchildren. Your kindness, wisdom, and presence brought joy and strength to our family.

I will deeply miss your companionship. I will miss our conversations, our shared laughter, and the comfort of having you by my side. The house feels so quiet without you, and words cannot fully express the emptiness your absence has left behind.

Though it hurts that I will no longer see you here with us, my consolation is that you are resting peacefully with your Creator. I hold on to the hope that one day we shall meet again, where there will be no more pain, sorrow, or parting.

Adieu, my beloved husband. Sleep well and rest in perfect peace.

Amen.
excerpt: To My Dearest Husband, Sir (Pharm.) Femi Francis Ogini KSM, FPSN Your exit came as a great shock and a deeply painful one. Yet, I give Go...

author: ENGR. OLABISI MOJOYINOLA
message:
TRIBUTE TO A BELOVED FATHER-IN-LAW

Daddy was more than a father-in-law to me; he was truly a father in every sense of the word. His love, guidance, prayers, and wise counsel were a constant source of strength to me and his grandchildren. He lived a life worthy of emulation — disciplined, humble, simple, peaceful, and deeply devoted to the service of God.He was a man of strong Christian faith who served the Lord wholeheartedly and raised his children in the fear and knowledge of God. His life reflected kindness, integrity, patience, and selfless sacrifice. He believed strongly in family unity, moral uprightness, and hard work, values he diligently passed on to his children and everyone around him.Daddy’s prayers, encouragement, and fatherly advice will forever remain fresh in our hearts. Though his sudden departure has left a painful vacuum in the family, we are comforted by the beautiful memories he left behind and the impactful life he lived.We thank God for the gift of his life, for the legacy of faith and love he deposited in his family, and for the many lives he touched positively during his lifetime.Sir, you came, you lived well, you loved sincerely, and you served God faithfully.We will greatly miss your fatherly presence, your words of wisdom, and your ever caring heart.May the Almighty God grant your gentle soul eternal rest and give the entire family the strength to bear this irreparable loss.Rest on, Daddy, in the bosom of the Lord until we meet again.	Engr. Olabisi Mojoyinola

author: Mojoyinola Abiola Michael
message:
Dear Sir Femi Ogini 

Grandpa, your passing has left a deep pain in my heart, but we are grateful for the beautiful life you lived and the legacy you left behind. You were a man of wisdom, strength, kindness, and honor. Your love for family and the values you stood for will never be forgotten.

As your grandson and the first child of your daughter, I am proud to have come from you. Thank you for your prayers, guidance, and the impact you made in our lives.

Though you are no longer with us, your memory will live on forever in our hearts.

Rest peacefully, Grandpa.

Your Grandson,
Mojoyinola Abiola Michael

I will never forget you. I love you grandpa. I miss you so much

author: Fr. Emeka Augustine Ugwumgbo
message:
*My Unforgettable Moment with Pa Ogini*

One Sunday morning after Mass, I was observing the lovely custom of exchanging pleasantries with parishioners. I noticed Pa Ogini in his car, clearly wanting to speak with me, so I went over at once.  

But I wasn’t prepared for what happened next. After I greeted him, he asked me a question no one had asked since my ordination — one I had never deeply considered myself. He said, “If you weren’t a priest, what other profession would you have chosen?”  

I was completely thrown off balance. Foolishly, I didn’t pause to think or even grasp where he was coming from. I stammered, “I love the sciences,” then fumbled through an answer I can no longer recall.  

What became etched in my memory in that split second was his response:  
“You wouldn’t have done as well in any other profession as you are doing in the priesthood.”  

Unfortunately, I never had the chance to tell him how deeply that moment impressed itself upon me.😭😭😭😭😭  
My testimony is this: he is a friend of Jesus and a friend of friends of Jesus.  He left us during this sacred season of the Resurrection — this Eastertide. He has gone to Galilee, as the Master directed on that first Easter morning, to meet the Lord.  

Adieu, Pharm!
May the angels lead you into the bosom of Abraham. 
Fr. Emeka prays for your happy repose.
Rev. Fr. Emeka Augustine Ugwumgbo, Bangor University, Wales, the UK.

message:
A rare man of honour, humility, wisdom, and faith. Sir Francis Femi Ogini lived a life that touched hearts, inspired many, and reflected true service to God and humanity. His legacy will continue through the lives he impacted, the values he stood for, and the love he gave so freely.

May his gentle soul rest in perfect peace. Gone from our sight, but never from our hearts.

author: Bọla Ọladokun
message:
Sir Francis Femi Ogini was more than a mentor in the knighthood - he was a guiding light whose life reflected discipline, service, and unwavering faith. A father figure in the Pharmacy profession, he nurtured minds, shaped careers, and inspired excellence in all who crossed his path. His leadership was firm yet compassionate, his counsel wise and timely. A true soldier of Christ, he lived a life of purpose, leaving behind footprints of integrity, kindness, and devotion. His legacy will continue to speak in the lives he touched. 
We take solace in knowing that he has gone to rest in the bosom of the Lord he served so faithfully.
May his noble soul rest in peace. Amen. 

~ Bọ́lá Ọládòkun KSM, FPCPHARM

author: Duru IK
message:
Mr. Ogini…
A quiet observer in a noisy world,
You move like the breeze
Unannounced, yet deeply felt.
You ask, “How is everyone?”
As though it’s just a question,
But we know
It is care dressed in simplicity.
You pretend not to notice much,
Yet you carry our names like sacred verses,
Calling each one without missing a line
And we stand there, amazed,
Wondering how a man so calm
Can remember hearts so vividly.
I remember that day on the road,
You parked, chasing small things for the house
Life’s simple errands.
I came to greet you,
Thinking it would be brief…
But you reached into the past
And pulled out a memory I had almost buried.
"How about that girlfriend?" you asked,
As though time never moved.
I said, “We broke up.”
And you, with the wisdom of quiet mountains, replied
"Don’t worry… she didn’t deserve you.
If she did, she would have known
You were trying to build something real."
Two years gone…
Yet your words still stand like pillars.
Who does that?
Who remembers like that?
Who cares like that?
Sometimes I ask myself
Are you not a saint in disguise?
Because men like you are rare…
Men who see beyond the surface,
Men who speak life without noise.
And then I look at Kowas…
And I laugh
“See this baba werey last born…”
Life has its own humor,
Even in the shadow of greatness.
But truth be told,
He came from you
And that alone says something divine.
We will miss you, Daddy.
Not just your presence
But your essence.
The way you made people feel seen,
The way you turned small moments into lasting echoes,
The way your words stayed long after you left.
We will celebrate you
Not with tears alone,
But with stories, laughter, and gratitude.
Because a life like yours
Is not mourned in silence
It is honored in fullness.
You were a good man.
A rare man.
A man who lived meaningfully.
And in our hearts,
You will never be forgotten.
Duru Ikemdinachukwu Emmanuel Josemaria
